S38: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. A B S T R A C T Background Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is terminal, progressive neurological condition for which there are no curative treatments.
S39: Among people with ALS/MND, fatigue is a common and debilitating symptom, which is characterised by reversible motor weakness and whole-body tiredness that is only partially relieved by rest.
S40: The eﬀectiveness of pharmacological or non- pharmacological treatments for fatigue in ALS/MND is not yet established.
S41: Objectives To assess the eﬀects of pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions for fatigue in ALS/MND.
S42: Search methods We searched the following databases on 5 September 2017: Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register, CENTRAL, MEDLINE, Embase, PsycINFO, CINAHL Plus, and ERIC.
S43: We also searched two clinical trials registries.
S44: Selection criteria We selected randomised and quasi-randomised controlled trials of any intervention which sought to reduce fatigue for people with ALS/ MND.
S45: We included studies if reduction in fatigue was a primary or secondary outcome of the trial.
S46: Data collection and analysis We used the standard methodological procedures expected by Cochrane.
S47: Main results We included one pharmacological (modafinil) study and three non-pharmacological studies (resistance exercise, respiratory exercise, and repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS)), involving a total of 86 participants with ALS/MND.
S48: None of the included studies were free from risk of bias.
S49: Since there was only one trial for each intervention, no meta-analysis was possible.
S50: All studies assessed fatigue using the Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS; scale from 9 to 63, higher scores indicate more fatigue).
S51: Information for assessing bias was ofen lacking in study reports, making the risk of bias unclear across several domains in all trials.
S52: Blinding of participants was not possible in exercise trials, but the outcome assessment was blinded.

S57: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews We found very low-quality evidence suggesting possible improvements in fatigue for modafinil treatment versus placebo (MD -11.00, 95% CI -23.08 to 1.08), respiratory exercise versus a sham intervention (MD -9.65, 95% CI -22.04 to 2.73), and rTMS versus sham rTMS (data not provided), which warrant further investigation to clarify the eﬀicacy of these treatments for fatigue in ALS/MND.
S58: We found no clear improvements in fatigue for resistance exercise versus usual care (MD 0.20, 95% CI -10.98 to 11.38; very low-quality evidence).
S59: Three participants in the modafinil group dropped out of the modafinil study, two citing issues with headache and one with chest tightness; other adverse eﬀects were anxiety, nausea, dizziness, and sialorrhoea (probably ALS-related).
S60: The trials reported no adverse eﬀects of exercise or rTMS.
S61: We cannot be certain about the eﬀects of any of the interventions studied because of imprecision (small numbers of participants, wide CI), and possible study limitations.
S62: Authors' conclusions It is impossible to draw firm conclusions about the eﬀectiveness of interventions to improve fatigue for people with ALS/MND as there are few randomised studies, and the quality of available evidence is very low.
S63: P L A I N L A N G U A G E S U M M A R Y Treatments for extreme tiredness and lack of energy (fatigue) in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease Review question What are the eﬀects of treatments for fatigue in people living with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) compared to no treatment or placebo?
S64: Background ALS, which is also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is a condition in which the nerves that control movement stop working.
S65: People experience problems moving their limbs, maintaining posture, swallowing, and breathing, which worsen over time and shorten life.
S66: The cause is unknown, and there is no cure.
S67: People living with ALS/MND ofen experience fatigue, which can cause distress and reduce quality of life.
S68: Fatigue can have many causes, including respiratory problems, medication, malnutrition, and depression.
S69: Our focus in this review was on treatments for fatigue that arises from the condition itself.
S70: Diﬀerent treatments may improve symptoms of fatigue in ALS/MND.
S71: These include medicines, which may help people feel more awake, and other treatments, such as exercise.
S72: It is unclear whether any of these treatments are eﬀective for improving fatigue in ALS/MND.
S73: We reviewed the available studies on the eﬀects of treatments for fatigue in ALS/MND.
S74: Study characteristics The review included four small studies with a total of 86 participants.
S75: Each study investigated a diﬀerent treatment.
S76: These were a drug treatment (modafinil) compared to placebo, breathing exercises compared to sham (inactive) breathing exercises, exercises with weights compared to usual care, and magnetic brain stimulation compared to sham rTMS.
S77: Key results and quality of the evidence We are very uncertain about the eﬀects of modafinil, breathing exercises, exercises with weights, or magnetic brain stimulation on fatigue in people with ALS/MND, as the evidence was very low quality.
S78: It was ofen unclear whether studies were adequately designed and performed, as trial reports ofen lacked details.
S79: The results of these small studies were not precise.
S80: Three participants stopped taking modafinil because of side eﬀects: headache in two, and chest tightness in one; participants also reported anxiety, nausea, dizziness, and sialorrhoea (inability to control oral secretions).
S81: We need more research on eﬀective treatments for fatigue in ALS/MND.
S82: The searches are up to date to September 2017.

S137: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews B A C K G R O U N D Fatigue is a commonly reported symptom in people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease (ALS/MND) (Ramirez 2008), with a large proportion of people reporting 'clinically significant' levels of fatigue (McElhiney 2009).
S138: Fatigue in ALS/MND has been defined as "reversible motor weakness and whole-body tiredness that was predominantly brought on by muscular exertion and was partially relieved by rest" (Gibbons 2013a).
S139: In ALS/MND, fatigue is distinguished from sleepiness by feelings of weariness or exhaustion that do not necessarily beget a desire to sleep.
S140: This fatigue appears to be experienced predominantly as general (feelings of whole-body tiredness) and physical (reversible motor weakness).
S141: People with other neurological diseases, such as multiple sclerosis, describe an experience of general physical fatigue occurring alongside cognitive fatigue (reversible reduction in concentration or mental performance)(Lou 2003; Mills 2008).
S142: In ALS/MND, fatigue can be experienced as a pervasive feeling of tiredness or lethargy, or as an objective decline in the ability of a muscle to contract to maximum force (Gibbons 2011; Lou 2012).
S143: The first type of fatigue is ubiquitous in humans, and may have been experienced by the individual before diagnosis; however, following development of ALS/MND, the severity of this fatigue and precipitating factors substantially change.
S144: The second type of fatigue, objective motor fatigue, also known as physical fatigability, is unique to the disease state and is not normally within a person’s previous experience.
S145: Both types of fatigue have been shown to be important to people with ALS/MND (Gibbons 2011), and fatigue, more generally, impacts negatively upon quality of life (Lou 2003).
S146: Evidence for the relationship between fatigue and functional ability is conflicting.
S147: Although some studies have suggested that fatigue is related to functional capacity (Lo Coco 2012; McElhiney 2009), others have been unable to support this claim (Gibbons 2011; Ramirez 2008).
S148: Fatigue appears to be related to common nighttime complaints in ALS/MND, including muscle cramps and nocturia (Lo Coco 2012).
S149: Fatigue is related to psychological distress, social withdrawal, and reduced quality of life for people with ALS/MND (Gibbons 2013b; Lou 2003).
S150: Description of the condition ALS/MND is a progressive, terminal neurodegenerative disease of unknown aetiology that is currently without a cure.
S151: The incidence of ALS/MND is around 2.16 per 100,000 person-years (Logroscino 2010).
S152: At any one time, approximately 5000 people in the United Kingdom are aﬀected (Shaw 1999), and 25,000 in North America (McGuire 1996).
S153: Rapid progression of the disease causes weakness and muscular atrophy, impacting upon an individual's ability to carry out activities of daily living, such as dressing, bathing, and eating.
S154: Problems with speech and swallowing ofen occur as the result of weakness in bulbar musculature.
S155: As the disease progresses, breathing issues (including nocturnal hypoventilation and reduced sleep quality) become apparent, and may progress until respiratory failure occurs (Bourke 2004).
S156: Death has been reported in most people with MND within two to five years following diagnosis, usually from respiratory failure, caused by respiratory muscle weakness (Rowland 2001).
S157: Description of the intervention Currently, no evidence-based treatment is available for fatigue in people with ALS/MND.
S158: Several drugs have been investigated as treatment for fatigue in this population, including amantadine, pemoline, and bupropion, although evidence regarding their eﬀicacy is lacking (Jackson 2006).
S159: Modafinil, a novel wakefulness- promoting agent that has been approved for the treatment of excessive sleepiness associated with narcolepsy, may also be an eﬀective treatment for fatigue (Carter 2005; McElhiney 2009; Rabkin 2009).
S160: Dietary supplementation of creatine may increase, or at least preserve, muscular strength, and reduce levels of fatigue (Rosenfeld 2008).
S161: Other non-pharmacological therapies may be used to ameliorate fatigue.
S162: Studies have evaluated the potential benefits of supported treadmill ambulation (Sanjak 2010), muscular exercise (Drory 2001), and repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) (Guo 2012; Zanette 2008) for this purpose.
S163: How the intervention might work Pharmacological interventions that stimulate the central nervous system (CNS) may reduce generalised fatigue for people with ALS/MND.
S164: Modafinil stimulates the release of norepinephrine and dopamine from synaptic terminals and elevates hypothalamic levels of histamine (Ishizuka 2008).
S165: Creatine, which increases maximum availability for energy output in anaerobic activities, may also have a positive eﬀect on muscle strength and fatigue (Ellis 2004; Kley 2013; Rosenfeld 2008).
S166: Diﬀerent treatments may be eﬀective for diﬀerent forms of fatigue.
S167: Non-pharmacological interventions may focus on light exercise, including supported or unsupported exercises (e.g.
S168: walking), or resistance training using weights (Bello-Haas 2007).
S169: Why it is important to do this review Fatigue is prevalent in people with ALS/MND and has a significant impact on quality of life.
S170: Presently, clarity regarding the best management of fatigue in this population is lacking.
S171: This review is the first systematic review of pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions for fatigue in ALS/MND.
S172: The purpose of this review is to identify interventions to reduce primary fatigue, which is disease related.
S173: We will not consider secondary causes of fatigue, such as malnutrition and chronic respiratory failure, which have been assessed in other Cochrane systematic reviews (Katzberg 2011; Radunovic 2017).

S180: Types of participants We included studies in which study participants were diagnosed with possible, probable, or definite ALS/MND, according to recognised criteria, preferably the El Escorial criteria (Brooks 2000).
S181: Types of interventions We included all interventions that aimed to reduce fatigue in people with ALS/MND, and measured it as either a primary or a secondary objective.
S182: These pharmacological or non- pharmacological treatments could be compared to each other, placebo, or standard care.

S185: Types of outcome measures Primary outcomes The primary outcome was level of fatigue at the end of the follow- up period.
S186: Fatigue could be evaluated using any validated patient- or clinician- administered fatigue questionnaire that measures general fatigue, including the Modified Fatigue Impact Scale (MFIS) (Fisk 1994) and the Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS) (Krupp 1989).
S187: We also included questionnaires that measured general fatigue and reversible muscle weakness, such as the Neurological Fatigue Index–MND (NFI-MND) (Gibbons 2011).
S188: Secondary outcomes Secondary outcomes were assessed at the end of the follow-up period, and could include the following.
S190: Sleepiness of participants measured by a validated scale including the Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS) (Johns 1991).
S192: Depression measured by a validated scale or by a clinical diagnostic interview, including the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) (Zigmond 1983), or the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) (Beck 1988).
S194: Quality of life of participants measured by a validated objective or subjective instrument, including the ALS Specific Quality of Life-Revised (ALSSQOL-R) (Felgoise 2008), the McGill Quality of Life Questionnaire (Cohen 1995), or the Short Form-36 Health Survey (SF-36) (Ware 1992).
S196: Functional status of participants measured by a validated scale such as the ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised (ALSFRS-R) (Cedarbaum 1999).
S199: Search methods for identification of studies Electronic searches We searched the following databases on 5 September 2017.
S200: • Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register and Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) via the Cochrane Register of Studies (CRS-Web); (searched 5 September 2017) using the search strategy in Appendix 1 • MEDLINE (1966 to August 2017) using the search strategy in Appendix 2.
S201: • Embase (1980 to August 2017) using the search strategy in Appendix 3.
S202: • PsycINFO (1806 to August 2017) using the search strategy in Appendix 4.
S203: • CINAHL Plus (Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature Plus; 1937 to August 2017) using the search strategy in Appendix 5.
S204: • ERIC (1966 to August 2017) using the search strategy in Appendix 6 Searching other resources We scanned available conference abstracts from International ALS/MND Symposia for relevant studies.
S205: We checked all references in the identified trials, and contacted trial authors to identify additional published or unpublished data.
S206: We searched trial registries (US National Institutes of Health trials registry, ClinicalTrials.gov (clinicaltrials.gov/), and the World Health Organization International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (ICTRP; apps.who.int/trialsearch/)) to identify ongoing trials.
S207: We considered trials published in languages other than English to be eligible for inclusion, following translation.
S208: Data collection and analysis Selection of studies Three review authors (CAY, CG, and FP) checked titles and abstracts identified during the electronic searches.
S209: These review authors obtained the full text of all potentially relevant studies for independent assessment.
S210: All review authors independently assessed which trials fit the inclusion criteria.
S211: We resolved disagreements about inclusion criteria by discussion and consensus.
S212: We excluded duplicate reports, and collated multiple reports of the same study, so that each study, rather than each report, was the unit of interest in the review.
S213: We recorded the selection process in suﬀicient detail to complete a PRISMA flow diagram and a 'Characteristics of excluded studies' table.
S214: Data extraction and management Two review authors (CG and FP) independently extracted data onto a specially designed form; the other two review authors (CAY and TF) checked the data extraction.
S215: One review author entered the data into the sofware (CG), another (FP) checked the entered data.
S216: Where data allowed, we extracted the following study characteristics.
S218: Methods: study design, total duration of study, details of any 'run-in' period, number of study centres and locations, study settings, withdrawals, and date of study.
S220: Participants: number (N), mean age, age range, gender, severity of condition, diagnostic criteria, baseline characteristics, inclusion criteria, and exclusion criteria.
S222: Interventions: intervention, comparison, concomitant medications, and excluded medications.
S224: Outcomes: primary and secondary outcomes specified and collected, and time points reported.
S226: Notes: funding for trial, and notable conflicts of interest of trial authors.

S231: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Assessment of risk of bias in included studies Two review authors (CG and FP) independently assessed risk of bias for each study using the Cochrane 'Risk of bias' tool, as described in Chapter 8 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins 2011).
S232: All review authors discussed disagreements related to risk of bias, and reached a consensus.
S233: We assessed risk of bias according to the following domains.
S248: We graded studies as having high, low, or unclear risk of bias in each of these domains, and provided justifications for these judgements in the 'Risk of bias' table in Characteristics of included studies.
S249: Measures of treatment eﬀect If suﬀicient studies had been included, we would have evaluated treatment eﬀects for continuous outcomes using mean diﬀerences (MDs), or standardised mean diﬀerences (SMDs) for results across studies with outcomes that were conceptually the same, but measured in diﬀerent ways.
S250: In the event that studies presented dichotomous data (e.g.
S251: responder analyses), we would have used risk ratios (RRs).
S252: We would have calculated 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the measures of treatment eﬀect.
S253: We would have undertaken meta-analyses only where meaningful, that is, when treatments, participants, and the underlying clinical questions were similar enough for pooling to make sense.
S254: Assessment of reporting biases If we had identified a large number of eligible studies, we would have analysed a funnel plot to assess the potential existence of small-study bias.
S255: Data synthesis We had planned to use the random-eﬀects model for the summary eﬀect measure in any meta-analysis.
S256: The weights for the studies would have been be inverse to the variances.
S257: The random-eﬀects model incorporates possible between-study variation as well as within-study diﬀerences, and so is more conservative.
S258: The fixed- eﬀect model assumes that no between-study diﬀerences exist.
S259: Both models yield very similar results, unless significant between-study diﬀerences are noted (heterogeneity).
S260: 'Summary of findings' table We created 'Summary of findings' tables, using fatigue as the outcome.
S261: We used the five GRADE considerations (study limitations, inconsistency of eﬀect, imprecision, indirectness, and publication bias) to assess the quality of a body of evidence (studies that contributed data for the outcome).
S263: Since we included only RCTs and quasi-RCTs in this review, we started from an assumption of high-quality evidence, from which we downgraded the quality to moderate, low, or very low, based on the extent to which the GRADE considerations presented a threat to validity.
S264: We used methods and recommendations described in Chapter 12 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Schünemann 2011) and GRADEpro sofware (GRADEpro 2008).
S265: We justified all decisions to downgrade or upgrade the quality of studies using footnotes, with comments to aid readers' understanding of the review when necessary.
S266: Subgroup analysis and investigation of heterogeneity We would have assessed the level of functional impairment, measured using the ALSFRS-R (Cedarbaum 1999), to identify heterogeneity of participants.
S267: If numbers had allowed, we would have used this scale to create subgroups of participants to analyse separately and compare.
S268: Sensitivity analysis If a study was of doubtful eligibility for the systematic review, appeared to be an outlier, or had missing data that were impossible to retrieve, we had intended to compare the results of analyses with and without the trial.

S430: D I S C U S S I O N Summary of main results We included four studies (86 participants) that met the eligibility criteria in this review.
S431: They evaluated pharmacological interventions (modafinil) (Rabkin 2009) and non-pharmacological interventions, including resistance exercise (Dal Bello Haas 2007), respiratory exercise (Pinto 2012), and repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) (Zanette 2008) We are very uncertain about the eﬀects of these interventions on fatigue in ALS, as the quality of evidence was too low to rule out any negative eﬀect or establish clinical benefit.
S432: Rabkin 2009 (32 participants) conducted an ITT analysis that showed improved fatigue at the end of a brief double-blind phase for the modafinil group, although the 95% CI were wide, and allowed for the possibility of no eﬀect.
S433: Resistance exercise had little eﬀect on fatigue in Dal Bello Haas 2007 (27 participants).
S434: In Pinto 2012 (26 participants), there was reduced fatigue in the respiratory exercise group over the sham intervention group, but the results were imprecise and 95% CI were consistent with both a large eﬀect and none.
S435: No clear diﬀerences between groups were reported for sleepiness, quality of life, or functional status, but there was small reduction of depressive symptoms in the respiratory exercise group.
S436: Zanette 2008 (10 participants) reported that improvements in fatigue in the rTMS group showed a "trend towards" statistical significance before post hoc tests corrected for multiple comparisons.
S437: These eﬀects were short-lived, and afer two weeks with no treatment, the trialists reported that there was no clear diﬀerence between the two groups on any of the reported outcomes.
S438: In the modafinil study, three participants dropped out from the intervention arm due to side eﬀects including headache and headache with chest tightness.
S439: The trial authors noted that similar adverse events were not reported in another RCT they had run with over 100 people with HIV/AIDS (Rabkin 2009).
S440: Similarly, an open-label trial of modafinil for ALS/MND reported that the drug was well tolerated in both the 200 mg and 400 mg groups (Carter 2005).
S441: Rabkin and colleagues also ran a completer's analysis, in which the positive eﬀect of modafinil in alleviating fatigue was more pronounced, with eﬀect sizes significant at the 5% level for fatigue, sleepiness, energy, and stamina (Rabkin 2009).
S442: No adverse events were reported in any of the non- pharmacological trials.
S443: Overall completeness and applicability of evidence We identified four studies.
S444: Each evaluated diﬀerent interventions which, at best, reported weak or transient beneficial eﬀects.
S445: The heterogeneity in the assessed interventions precluded meta- analysis of the disparate outcomes.
S446: Therefore, no reliable information was available to assess whether diﬀerent treatments for fatigue may be beneficial or harmful for people with ALS/MND.
S447: This review highlights a clear need to commit high-quality evidence to the corpus of literature relating to treatment for fatigue in people with ALS/MND.
S448: Quality of the evidence We assessed the overall risk of bias from low to unclear for two of the four studies.
S449: We found issues relating to blinding of participants or personnel in two of the trials.
S450: In Pinto 2012, the trainer was aware of the group allocation and in Dal Bello Haas 2007, the participants were unblinded due to the nature of the intervention.
S451: Though they were asked not to reveal their allocation to the assessors, no formal evaluation took place to confirm that assessors remained unblinded throughout the trial period.
S452: High dropout introduced a high risk of bias for the study by Rabkin and colleagues of modafinil versus placebo (Rabkin 2009).
S453: It is notable that more than half of the risk of bias domains could not be ascertained from the published reports.
S454: All results lacked precision and were based on findings from single small studies.
S455: There is an additional risk that we were unable to identify all relevant controlled research studies, due to publication bias.
S456: Potential biases in the review process We followed the Cochrane Neuromuscular search strategies, which include a search of the Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register, which is updated weekly to monthly from a range of databases.
S457: Therefore, it is unlikely that studies have been missed, although it is possible that studies that have not been published could be missing.
S458: Should any further studies be identified, we will include them in future updates of the review.
S459: We followed the recommended Cochrane review process to reduce potential biases, which included having at least two review authors independently assess identified studies, extract data, and evaluate risk of bias.
S460: The small size of included studies means that estimates of adverse event rate frequency are unlikely to be accurate, particularly for rare events.

S465: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Agreements and disagreements with other studies or reviews To our knowledge, there are no other systematic reviews on this topic for people with ALS/MND.
S466: A U T H O R S ' C O N C L U S I O N S Implications for practice There is very limited and low-quality evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) about treatment to reduce fatigue in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease (ALS/MND).
S467: Therefore, it is uncertain whether modafinil, breathing exercises, resistance exercise, or repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) are of benefit.
S468: Implications for research Despite the prevalence of fatigue in ALS/MND, we lack high-quality RCTs that evaluate interventions to improve this disabling, but potentially treatable symptom.
S469: There is a need for considerable further work to identify an eﬀective treatment for fatigue for people with ALS/MND.
S470: Three studies demonstrated very low quality evidence of benefit for modafinil, inspiratory muscle training, and rTMS, which may merit further investigation.
S471: Unfortunately, the positive eﬀects of rTMS were short lived, and no longer detected two weeks afer treatment cessation.
S472: Although the eﬀect of modafinil and respiratory exercise is very uncertain, as the quality of the evidence is very low, they appear to have the greatest potential as an eﬀective treatment for fatigue in ALS/MND.
